Course, no bother. We go up the east side, and we have to be back down by three. I talk fast, so cut in any time.
Thank you. So what you're saying is, we go up, then back by three. Do you mean three o'clock sharp?
Exactly, three sharp, before the light goes. See, you are following me grand. Most folk just nod and get lost halfway up.
That's my new trick. If I lose the thread, I just ask, instead of pretending. It works much better.

Hi Marcelo, it is Saoirse from the glacier tour, just leaving you a quick message. So, tomorrow we are gonna meet down at the harbor at eight, not at the hotel, okay? I know I talk fast, so do not worry if you do not catch every single word. Here is the important bit. You are gonna wanna bring proper boots and a warm jacket, because up on the ice it gets cold really fast. We hafta be back before dark, so we will set off early. If the weather turns, I will give you a shout and we will move the whole thing to the afternoon. Oh, and one more thing. When we are up there and I am pointing at things, if you lose the thread, just cut in and ask me to say it again. Honestly, everybody does. See you at eight. Bye now.

Welcome back to the show. Today, why native speakers are so hard to understand, and what you can actually do about it. Here is the thing most learners get wrong. They think the problem is vocabulary. Usually, it is not. The problem is speed and connection. When we speak fast, we do not say want to, we say wanna. We do not say going to, we say gonna. Whole words get swallowed. So a sentence you could read easily on paper becomes a wall of sound. What is the fix? First, train your ear on real material, not slow textbook audio. Watch a documentary, follow a panel discussion, listen to the live commentary of a match you actually care about. Second, and this is the big one, stop pretending. When you do not catch something, do not just nod and smile. Cut in, politely, and ask. Say, sorry, could you say that again, or, do you mean this or that. Every strong listener you admire does exactly that. They did not get a magic ear. They just stopped being afraid to ask.
He spoke so fast that I lost the thread.
Sorry, I could not catch your last name.
If you do not understand, just cut in and ask.
I train my ear with a documentary every week.

I watch a documentary with English subtitles.
Do you mean this or that?
